title: Lamb dhansak
subtitle: Lamb and lentil curry with a sweet-sour depth
excerpt: Lamb shoulder simmered until tender in a sauce built from red and yellow lentils, pumpkin, and a blend of Parsi spices. The lentils dissolve into the sauce to create a thick, iron-rich gravy; the pumpkin adds a quiet sweetness; the tamarind gives the characteristic sour note. One of the most substantial curries on the British Indian menu.
sourceNotes: Dhansak is a Parsi dish of Persian origin, brought to India by the Zoroastrian diaspora. It uses a distinctive sweet-sour-hot balance from tamarind and jaggery alongside the lentil base. The British curry-house version is a simplified adaptation, documented in restaurant literature from the 1970s.

content.text: Dhansak is a long-cook dish: the lamb needs at least 75 minutes to become properly tender, and the lentils need time to dissolve completely into the sauce. Don't rush either stage. The correct consistency is thick, the lentils should not be distinguishable as individual pieces; they become the gravy. If the sauce is still identifiably lentil-chunky, cook for longer.

The sweet-sour balance is the defining characteristic: tamarind provides the sour note, and a small amount of sugar or jaggery counters it. Taste at the end and adjust, both elements should be present but neither should dominate. The lentil base mutes both, so season assertively.
Brown the lamb and build the base

Heat the {{ghee}} in a large, heavy pan over high heat. Brown the {{lamb-shoulder}} in batches for 2 to 3 minutes per batch, don't crowd the pan. Remove and set aside. Reduce the heat to medium. Add the {{onion}} and cook for 12 minutes until deep golden. Add the {{garlic}}, {{ginger-root}}, {{turmeric}}, {{cumin-ground}}, {{coriander-ground}}, and {{chilli-powder}} and stir for 1 minute. Add the {{tinned-tomatoes}} and cook for 5 minutes until reduced slightly.
Cook the lentils and lamb together

Return the lamb to the pan. Add the {{red-lentils}}, {{butternut-squash}}, and 600 ml water. Bring to the boil, then reduce to a low simmer, cover, and cook for 75 minutes, stirring occasionally, until the lamb is tender and the lentils have dissolved. Add the {{tamarind-paste}}, {{brown-sugar}}, and {{garam-masala}}. Stir well and season with salt. Taste and adjust the sweet-sour balance, add more tamarind for sourness or a pinch more sugar to balance. Simmer uncovered for a further 5 minutes to reduce to a thick, coating sauce.

items.fix: Cook uncovered on medium heat, stirring frequently, until thickened. Mash any remaining lentil pieces against the side of the pan
items.cause: Lentils not fully dissolved or not reduced enough

items.fix: Continue cooking, dhansak can take up to 2 hours for cheaper cuts. Ensure the liquid is at a visible simmer, not just warm
items.cause: Temperature too low or pieces cut too large

items.fix: Add a pinch more sugar and a splash of water. The sweetness rounds the sourness without removing it
Lentil dishes need more salt than intuition suggests. Taste after the lentils have fully dissolved and season again then
items.cause: Lentils absorb a large amount of salt

content.text: Dhansak is one of the richer curries on the British curry-house menu. The mix of lamb, lentil, and squash makes it a properly filling main. It sits in the medium-hot range and has its own character. The Parsi influence gives it a sweet-sour edge that is worth ordering over a standard korma or madras. In Parsi cooking the dish is served with caramelised brown rice and never on Sundays, which is thought unlucky. The curry-house version drops both habits.
